docs.title: HelixAE
docs.text: HelixAE is a Prism pipeline plugin for Adobe After Effects. It connects AE directly to your Prism project, letting you track footage versions, import renders, manage compositions, and send shots to the render queue — all without leaving After Effects.

docs.title: Opening the Panel
docs.text:
HelixAE lives inside After Effects as a CEP panel. To open it:

  1. In After Effects, go to Window in the top menu
  2. Find HelixAE in the list
  3. Dock it wherever you like — most artists keep it on the side

docs.title: The CEP Panel
docs.text:
The panel is your main control hub. It contains the following buttons:

Button What it does Save Version Save the current AE project as a new Prism version Save Comment... Save a new version with an extended comment/note Project Browser... Open the Prism project browser Footage Tracker... Open the Footage Tracker dialog Import Media... Open the Import dialog to bring in renders Check Issues Quickly check for footage problems (outdated versions, FPS/frame range mismatches) Prism Settings... Open Prism settings Render... Open the Render dialog to queue a shot for rendering

docs.title: Typical Artist Workflow
docs.text:
1. Open AE and the HelixAE panel
  2. Use Footage Tracker to check if your renders are up to date
  3. Use Import Media to pull in new renders
  4. Work on your comp
  5. Use Render to add your shot to the render queue
  6. Use Save Version to save your progress

docs.title: Opening the Import Dialog
docs.text: Use the Import Media button in the CEP panel, or right-click empty space in the Footage Tracker, to open the Import dialog.

docs.title: Selecting a Shot
docs.text:
1. Click Choose... at the top of the dialog
  2. A shot browser will open — select the shot(s) you want to import from
  3. The dialog will populate with all available renders for that shot

docs.title: Filtering by Type
docs.text:
Use the type filter checkboxes to narrow down what's shown:

Filter What it shows 3D 3D renders (EXR sequences, AOV passes) 2D 2D renders (composited outputs, painted frames) PB Playblasts (animation previews)

All three are checked by default. Uncheck any you don't need.

docs.title: The Import Table
docs.text:
The table shows all available footage matching your filters:

Column Description Shot Shot name Type 3D / 2D / PB Task / Identifier The task or render identifier AOV AOV pass name (for 3D renders) Version Latest available version Status Shows ✓ if already imported into the current AE project

You can select multiple rows (Ctrl+click or Shift+click).

docs.title: Search
docs.text: Press Ctrl+Space to open the search bar. It filters across Shot, Type, Task, AOV, and Version columns. Wildcards are supported (*, ?).

docs.title: Importing
docs.text:
1. Select the rows you want
  2. Click Import Selected

The footage will be imported into your AE project and organized into the appropriate folders automatically.

docs.title: Adding Footage to a Composition
docs.text:
After importing, you can add any footage item directly to a composition from the Footage Tracker:

  1. Right-click the footage item
  2. Add to Comp → Current Comp — adds it to whichever comp you have open
  3. Add to Comp → Choose Comp... — opens a picker to select the target comp

This works on already-imported footage at any time, not just right after importing.

docs.title: What is Kitsu?
docs.text: Kitsu is the studio's hub for shot management, task tracking, reviews, and feedback. HelixAE connects to Kitsu to pull in shot data — frame ranges, FPS, task statuses — and display it directly in the Footage Tracker.

docs.title: What Data Comes from Kitsu
docs.text: Data Where it's used Shot frame range Shown in the Frame Range column; used to detect mismatches with your comp Shot FPS Shown in the FPS column; used to detect FPS mismatches Task status Displayed as a colour-coded indicator next to footage items Task name Shown in the tracker tree

docs.title: Status Colours
docs.text: Each footage item in the tracker can show a colour from Kitsu representing the task's current status (e.g. In Progress, Pending Review, Done). These colours come directly from your Kitsu project configuration.

docs.title: Refreshing Kitsu Data
docs.text:
Kitsu data is cached for 5 minutes to keep things fast. To refresh:

  • Click the ⟳ Refresh button in the tracker toolbar
    • Footage — reloads AE footage using cached Kitsu data (fast)
    • Kitsu — forces a fresh fetch from the Kitsu server (bypasses cache)

docs.title: Opening Shots & Tasks in Kitsu
docs.text:
Right-click any footage item in the tracker:

  • Open Shot in Kitsu — opens the shot page in your browser
  • Open Task in Kitsu — opens the specific task page in your browser

This is a quick way to jump to the review or leave a comment without leaving AE.

docs.title: Syncing from Kitsu
docs.text:
Right-click a footage item → Sync submenu:

Option What it does Version Updates footage to the latest version from Kitsu FPS Sets footage FPS to match Kitsu Frame Range Sets footage frame range to match Kitsu Resolution Sets footage resolution to match Kitsu

docs.title: Rendering a Shot
docs.text: When your comp is ready, use HelixAE to add it to the After Effects Render Queue.

docs.title: Opening the Render Dialog
docs.text: Click Render... in the CEP panel.

docs.title: The Render Dialog
docs.text: Field Description Template Select an output module template from the dropdown Add to Render Queue Saves the project and adds the current comp to the AE Render Queue Close Close the dialog without rendering

docs.title: Export Dialog
docs.text:
For Prism-managed export (saving as a versioned output), the Export dialog adds more options:

Field Description Task The Prism task this export belongs to Use next version Automatically increments to the next version number (on by default) Comment Optional comment saved with this export version Output Module Select which render template to use Local output Toggle whether output goes to local or network path

Click Add to Render Queue to save and queue the render.

docs.title: After Rendering
docs.text: Once After Effects finishes rendering, the output is saved as a new version in Prism. Other artists will see it in the Footage Tracker the next time they refresh.

docs.title: Keyboard Shortcuts
docs.text: Shortcut Action Ctrl+Space Toggle the search/filter bar (works in Footage Tracker and Import dialog) Ctrl+Shift+D Toggle the debug console in the Footage Tracker Ctrl+A Select all items in the tracker tree Delete Remove selected items from the tracker tree Enter Apply the current search filter Escape Close the search bar

docs.title: Search Tips
docs.text:
The search bar supports wildcards:

Pattern Matches beauty* Anything starting with "beauty" *_aux Anything ending with "_aux" sh0?0 sh010, sh020, sh030... [abc]* Anything starting with a, b, or c

Search filters across Shot, Identifier, AOV, and Version at the same time.

docs.title: Multi-Select
docs.text:
You can select multiple items in the tracker tree:

  • Ctrl+Click — add individual items to selection
  • Shift+Click — select a range
  • Right-click a multi-selection to apply actions to all selected items at once (e.g. update all, sync all)

docs.title: Always on Top
docs.text: Click the 📌 pin icon in the Footage Tracker title bar to keep the window above After Effects. This is useful when working between the tracker and your comps. The setting is saved between sessions.

docs.title: Refresh vs Kitsu Refresh
docs.text:
• Use Refresh → Footage for a fast reload (uses cached Kitsu data)
  • Use Refresh → Kitsu only when you know someone has updated a task or frame range in Kitsu and you need the latest data now

docs.title: Footage Tracker
docs.text:
The Footage Tracker is the main tool in HelixAE. It gives you a live overview of all footage in your AE project — what version each item is on, whether newer versions are available, and whether there are any FPS or frame range issues.

Open it from the CEP panel via Footage Tracker... or from the Prism menu.

Use the sections below to learn about each part of the tracker.

docs.title: What Are Issues?
docs.text: The Footage Tracker automatically checks your footage for common problems. When issues are found, the ⚠ Check Issues (N) button appears in the statistics bar showing the number of problems.

docs.title: What Gets Checked
docs.text: Issue Description Outdated version A newer version of this footage exists in Prism FPS mismatch The footage FPS doesn't match the shot FPS in Kitsu Frame range mismatch The footage frame range doesn't match the shot in Kitsu Resolution mismatch The footage resolution doesn't match expectations

docs.title: How to Use It
docs.text:
1. Open the Footage Tracker
  2. If the ⚠ Check Issues button appears, click it to scroll to and highlight the problematic footage
  3. Right-click the flagged item to see sync/update options

You can also trigger a manual check by clicking the Check Issues button in the CEP panel at any time, without opening the full tracker.

docs.title: Fixing Issues
docs.text: Issue Fix Outdated version Right-click → Sync → Version, or use Update All Outdated FPS mismatch Right-click → Sync → FPS Frame range mismatch Right-click → Sync → Frame Range Resolution mismatch Right-click → Sync → Resolution

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: The Footage Tracker also displays your compositions in the tree, grouped under the Comps section. You can manage them directly from the tracker.

docs.title: Right-Click Menu on a Composition
docs.text:
Right-click any composition in the tracker to get these options:

Action What it does Open Composition Opens the comp in the After Effects timeline Reveal in Project Selects the comp in the AE Project panel Show Info Displays composition metadata (resolution, FPS, frame range, duration) Set Frame Range from Kitsu Updates the comp's in/out points to match the shot's frame range in Kitsu Set FPS from Kitsu Updates the comp's FPS to match the shot's FPS in Kitsu Set From Kitsu Applies both frame range and FPS from Kitsu in one step Remove Unused Removes all comps and footage items not used by this composition Bypass / Unbypass Hides or shows the comp in the tracker tree

docs.title: Syncing Comps to Kitsu
docs.text:
If your shot's frame range or FPS has been updated in Kitsu, you can sync your comp in one click:

  1. Right-click the composition in the tracker
  2. Choose Set From Kitsu

This updates both the frame range and FPS of the comp to match what's in Kitsu.

docs.title: Remove Unused
docs.text:
Remove Unused is a cleanup tool. It scans the selected composition and removes any AE project items (comps, footage) that aren't referenced by it. Use this before rendering or archiving to keep the project clean.

Warning

This action cannot be undone. Make sure you have a saved version before running it.

docs.title: Opening the Footage Tracker
docs.text: Click Footage Tracker... in the CEP panel. The tracker window opens and loads all footage from the current AE project.

docs.title: The Tree
docs.text:
The main area of the tracker is a tree view showing all footage organised into groups:

  • 3D Renders — EXR sequences and AOV passes
  • 2D Renders — Composited outputs
  • Playblasts — Animation previews
  • External — Footage not managed by Prism
  • Comps — Your After Effects compositions

Each footage item shows the following columns:

Column Description Shot / Identifier / AOV The item name, organised hierarchically Version The version currently in your AE project Status Kitsu task status colour Frame Range Footage frame range (highlights red if mismatched) FPS Footage FPS (highlights red if mismatched) Resolution Footage resolution

docs.title: The Statistics Bar
docs.text:
At the bottom of the tracker, a bar shows:

  • Total footage count
  • Number of outdated versions
  • ⚠ Check Issues (N) button — appears when issues are detected (see Check Issues)

docs.title: The Refresh Button
docs.text:
Click the ⟳ Refresh dropdown to reload data:

  • Footage — fast reload using cached Kitsu data
  • Kitsu — force a fresh fetch from the Kitsu server

docs.title: Window Controls
docs.text:
• 📌 Always on Top — Pin the tracker above AE. Click again to unpin. State is saved between sessions.
  • 📁 Export Archive — Export a JSON file with all footage information (useful for archiving or reporting)

docs.title: Search Bar
docs.text: Press Ctrl+Space to open a floating search bar. Type to filter the tree by Shot, Identifier, AOV, or Version. Wildcards supported (*, ?). Press Escape to close.

docs.title: Navigating to Footage
docs.text:
Right-click any footage item for quick navigation:

  • Reveal in Project — Selects and highlights the item in the AE Project panel. Useful when you need to find where a render lives without searching manually.
  • Reveal in Compositions — Shows every composition in the project that uses this footage. Helpful before swapping a version so you know what will be affected.

docs.title: File Actions
docs.text:
Right-click any footage item for quick file access:

  • Open — Opens the footage file in its default application (e.g. DJV for EXR sequences)
  • Copy Path — Copies the full file path to your clipboard
  • Open in Explorer — Opens the containing folder in Windows Explorer

docs.title: Bypass / Unbypass
docs.text:
Bypass hides an item from the tracker tree without removing it from your AE project. Use it to declutter the view when you have reference footage or old items you're keeping but not actively working with.

Right-click any item → Bypass to hide it, Unbypass to bring it back.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: HelixAE can automatically organise items in your AE Project panel into a clean folder structure. This is useful when footage has been imported manually or things have gotten messy.

docs.title: How to Organise
docs.text:
1. In the Footage Tracker, right-click any folder (3D Renders, 2D Renders, Resources, Comps, or External)
  2. Select Organise...
  3. A preview dialog will appear showing the before/after folder structure
  4. Click Confirm to apply, or Cancel to abort

You can also right-click multiple folders at once and choose Organise All [N] Folders... to organise them in one step.

docs.title: Auto-Categorize Resources
docs.text:
For the Resources folder specifically, there's an additional option:

  • Right-click the Resources folder → Auto-Categorize

This automatically sorts loose resource items into subfolders based on their type (images, video, audio, etc.).

docs.title: Validate Naming Convention
docs.text: For 3D Renders and 2D Renders folders, you can right-click and choose Validate Naming Convention to check that all items follow the expected naming structure. Any inconsistencies will be reported.

docs.title: What is Resize AOV Images?
docs.text:
This tool batch-resizes an EXR image sequence to a smaller resolution. It's used to create proxy versions of heavy 3D render passes so After Effects can play them back faster during compositing.

Only appears on 3D render footage (EXR sequences).

docs.title: When Would You Use This?
docs.text:
• Your EXR sequences are 4K or larger and AE is slow to preview
  • You want to work on comp layout and timing before switching to full-res renders
  • You need a lightweight version of AOV passes to share or review without transferring full-res files

docs.title: How to Use It
docs.text:
1. Right-click a 3D footage item in the Footage Tracker
  2. Hover over Resize AOV Images
  3. Select the target resolution from the submenu
  4. The tool processes the image sequence and saves the resized version

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
Info

The resize runs using OpenImageIO, a professional image processing library. It handles EXR correctly, preserving colour space and channel data.

Warning

Resized images are saved alongside the originals, not overwriting them. This is a one-time batch operation — it does not create a live link. If new frames are rendered, you'll need to resize again.

docs.title: What is Shot Switch?
docs.text:
Shot Switch lets you redirect a footage item to the same render pass in a different shot, while keeping the identifier and AOV intact. HelixAE automatically picks the latest available version in the target shot.

This is different from Change Shot — here's the distinction:

Shot Switch Change Shot Use case Flip between shot variants (e.g. hero vs VFX shot) Point footage at a completely different shot Identifier/AOV Preserved automatically You choose manually Version Auto-selects latest You choose manually

docs.title: When Would You Use This?
docs.text:
Common scenarios:

  • A shot has a hero version and a VFX variant (e.g. sh010 and sh010_vfx) and you need to switch between them for a review
  • You're building a comp template that needs to work across multiple shots — switch the shot to test it
  • A shot was renamed or split and you need to redirect your existing comp to the new shot

docs.title: How to Use It
docs.text:
1. Right-click the footage item in the Footage Tracker
  2. Hover over Shot Switch
  3. The submenu lists all available alternative shots
  4. Click the target shot — HelixAE updates the footage path to the latest version of the same identifier/AOV in that shot

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
Info

Shot Switch only appears if HelixAE detects alternative shots with matching renders. If the submenu is empty or missing, no matching renders were found in other shots.

If you need to switch to a shot with a different identifier or AOV, use Change Shot instead (right-click → Change Shot), which gives you full manual control.

docs.title: Shot-First vs Identifier-First
docs.text:
For 3D Renders and 2D Renders, you can choose how footage is grouped in the tree:

  • Group by Shot (default) — top level is the shot name, then identifier/task underneath
  • Group by Identifier — top level is the identifier/task name, then shots underneath

This is a per-render-type setting, so you can have 3D Renders grouped one way and 2D Renders grouped another.

docs.title: How to Toggle
docs.text: Click the title bar of the render group (e.g. the "3D Renders" header row in the tree) to toggle between modes.

docs.title: When to Use Each Mode
docs.text: Mode Best for Group by Shot Working on a single shot — see all its renders together Group by Identifier Checking a specific render pass across multiple shots

docs.title: Why Update Versions?
docs.text: When a 3D artist renders a new version of a shot, your AE project still points to the old version. The Footage Tracker detects this and lets you update in a few clicks.

docs.title: Updating a Single Item
docs.text:
1. Right-click the outdated footage item in the tracker
  2. Go to Sync → Version
  3. The footage in your AE project is updated to the latest version

docs.title: Updating All Outdated at Once
docs.text:
If multiple items are outdated:

  1. Look at the statistics bar at the bottom of the tracker
  2. You'll see a count of outdated items
  3. Use Update All Outdated to update everything in one step

docs.title: Changing Shot or Identifier Manually
docs.text:
You can manually reassign footage to a different shot or task:

  • Right-click → Change Shot → pick from the list or choose Custom...
  • Right-click → Change 2D/3D Identifier → pick or type a custom name

Warning

After changing shot or identifier, the tracker will look for the matching render in the new location. Make sure the target render exists before changing.
